agent_output:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Early 200 ACK + synchronous 2MB XML parsing + zero error handling = silent data loss and event loop starvation under production load"
  },

  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "Early ACK guarantees silent data loss on any processing failure",
      "risk": "The endpoint sends 200 OK before doing ANY work. If the DB query fails, XML parsing throws, or the status update errors out, the carrier considers the webhook delivered and will never retry. Every failure is permanently lost data with zero signal — no log, no metric, no retry.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "Line 3: res.status(200).send('OK')",
      "reproduction": "1. Send a webhook with a valid trackingNumber. 2. Kill the DB connection after res.send() fires but before findByTrackingNumber completes. 3. Carrier gets 200, delivery never updates, no error is logged anywhere.",
      "fix": "Move res.status(200).send('OK') to AFTER successful processing, inside a try/catch. Return 500 on failure so the carrier retries. If carrier has a short timeout, queue the raw event to a durable store (Redis stream, SQS, DB table) before ACKing, then process asynchronously with retry logic.",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Delivery status accuracy and data integrity"
    },
    {
      "title": "Synchronous 2MB XML parsing blocks the Node.js event loop under load",
      "risk": "parseXmlSync on a 2MB payload can block the event loop for 50-200ms. At 200 webhooks/minute (~3.3/sec), overlapping sync parses will starve all other requests — health checks timeout, other webhooks queue up, and the process appears hung. This is the classic Node.js denial-of-service-by-CPU pattern.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "Line 10: const parsed = parseXmlSync(xmlData)",
      "reproduction": "1. Send 4 concurrent webhooks each with a 2MB XML payload. 2. Simultaneously send a health check request. 3. Observe health check latency spike to 400-800ms as the event loop is blocked by serial XML parsing.",
      "fix": "Replace parseXmlSync with an async XML parser (e.g., xml2js.parseStringPromise, fast-xml-parser's async mode, or offload to a worker thread via worker_threads). For payloads this size, a worker thread pool is the correct production pattern.",
      "time_to_fix": "20 minutes for async parser swap, 1 hour for worker thread pool",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Endpoint availability and latency SLAs"
    },
    {
      "title": "Zero error handling — unhandled promise rejection will crash the process",
      "risk": "After the response is sent, the remaining async work runs as a fire-and-forget promise. Any throw (DB down, malformed XML, null event.trackingNumber) becomes an unhandled promise rejection. In Node 15+, this terminates the process by default. Even if the process survives, the error is completely silent — no logging, no alerting, no retry.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "Entire handler body after res.send()",
      "reproduction": "1. Send a webhook with rawXmlPayload set to null or invalid XML. 2. parseXmlSync throws. 3. No catch handler exists. 4. Unhandled rejection fires. 5. In Node 15+, process exits. In older versions, error is silently swallowed.",
      "fix": "Wrap all post-ACK processing in a try/catch block. Log errors with full context (trackingNumber, error message, stack). Emit a metric for failed webhook processing. Add a dead-letter mechanism for failed events so they can be reprocessed.",
      "time_to_fix": "15 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "System reliability and observability"
    }
  ],

  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "Redundant and incorrect delivery lookup map",
      "description": "All deliveries returned by findByTrackingNumber(event.trackingNumber) share the same trackingNumber, so the map collapses them into a single entry — only the last delivery survives. If multiple delivery records share a tracking number, all but one silently get skipped. The map provides no performance benefit over the array and actively causes data loss.",
      "location": "Lines 7-9: deliveryMap construction"
    },
    {
      "title": "Sequential DB updates instead of batch/parallel",
      "description": "Each status update runs as a sequential await inside a for loop. With N updates per webhook and 200 webhooks/minute, this creates unnecessary DB round-trip latency. Should use Promise.all() or a batch UPDATE query.",
      "location": "Lines 13-17: for loop with await"
    },
    {
      "title": "No request body validation or size limit",
      "description": "event.trackingNumber and event.rawXmlPayload are used without null/type checks. A malformed webhook payload (missing fields, wrong types) will throw at an unpredictable point. No body size limit means a malicious payload larger than 2MB could amplify the sync parsing DoS.",
      "location": "Lines 5-6, Line 10"
    }
  ],

  "suggestions": [
    "Add an idempotency key (event ID) check before processing to handle carrier retries safely without double-updating",
    "Add request body size limit via express middleware: app.use(express.json({ limit: '3mb' }))",
    "Add structured logging with correlation IDs (trackingNumber, eventId) for production debugging",
    "Consider a queue-based architecture: ACK immediately after persisting the raw event to a durable queue, then process from the queue with retries and backpressure"
  ],

  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "Under 200 webhooks/min with 2MB payloads, the sync XML parsing will degrade all endpoints on the same process. Event loop starvation will cascade to health checks, causing orchestrators to restart the pod, making things worse.",
    "rollback_capability": "No deployment artifact to assess — this is a code review. The fixes are additive and backward-compatible.",
    "backward_compatibility": "Moving the 200 ACK after processing changes retry behavior from the carrier's perspective. Verify the carrier's retry policy and timeout before making this change.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "Zero observability — no logging, no metrics, no error tracking. A failure here is completely invisible.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": false,
      "data_pipeline": true,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": ["Delivery status updates are silently lost on any processing failure, corrupting downstream data pipeline state"]
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "N/A — this code should not ship in its current state. All three critical issues must be resolved first."
  },

  "escalations": [],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 95,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Event loop blocking from synchronous XML parsing is a well-known Node.js anti-pattern",
      "Fire-and-forget after early ACK guarantees silent data loss",
      "Missing error handling on detached async work causes unhandled rejections"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact parseXmlSync duration depends on the specific library and payload structure — 50-200ms is an estimate"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Node.js 15+ is in use (unhandled rejections terminate the process)",
      "The carrier API supports retry on non-2xx responses",
      "parseXmlSync is truly synchronous as its name implies"
    ]
  }
}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this webhook endpoint for critical issues.

Hypotheses:
1. Responding before processing means lost events on failure - no retry possible
2. Synchronous XML parsing of 2MB blocks the event loop
3. No error handling - any failure silently drops the update
4. Race conditions with concurrent webhooks for same tracking number
5. The deliveryMap is pointless - all deliveries have the same trackingNumber

Let me write the analysis.
